title: Microscopic mechanism of high-temperature superconductivity revealed by ab initio studies on hole-doped multilayer cuprates HgBa$_2$Ca$_2$Cu$_3$O$_8$ under pressure
abstract: Triple-layer cuprate superconductor $\mathrm{HgBa_2Ca_2Cu_3O_8}$ (Hg1223) keeps the record of the highest superconducting (SC) critical temperature $T_{c}\sim 134$K among all the existing materials at ambient pressure. $T_{c}$ further increases under pressure up to $T_{c}\sim 160$K. However, its microscopic mechanism remains to be elucidated. We solve {\it ab initio} Hamiltonians for Hg1223 using a variational solver supplemented by a neural network. The pressure dependence of the $d$-wave SC order parameter and estimated $T_{c}$ show a dome-like structure in essential agreement with the experimental indications. The origin of the strong SC amplitude at ambient pressure is identified as strong local Coulomb repulsion $U$ attributed to poor screening. Further increase in $T_{c}$ under pressure is understood from interplay of three elements, namely increased electron hopping $t$, decreased $U$ and more importantly, strongly reduced offsite Coulomb repulsion $V$ with increasing pressure. Pairing mechanism is identified as the emergent local attraction counterintuitively generated from the originally strong local repulsion $U$. The emergent attraction is interpreted from ``attraction from reduced repulsion'', originating from the release of the fluctuating doubly-occupied sites characterized from the ``false vacuum'' in the Mott insulator to the double-occupation-free $d$-wave SC states upon carrier doping. This instantaneous attraction is in contrast with the conventional BCS SC mediated by bosonic glues. The local attraction is consistent with the electron fractionalization supported in experimental analyses. The coexistence of the SC and antiferromagnetic order is also demonstrated as a characteristic feature of the multi-layer system. The microscopic understanding of Hg1223 offers a new route explicitly using this emergent attraction to design and optimize SC materials.
summary: This work uses ab initio calculations on $	ext{HgBa}_2	ext{Ca}_2	ext{Cu}_3	ext{O}_8$ to reveal that the mechanism for high-temperature superconductivity involves an emergent local attraction derived from strong Coulomb repulsion. The study successfully models the pressure dependence of $T_c$, showing it results from a complex interplay between hopping, onsite repulsion ($U$), and off-site repulsion ($V$). This provides a new theoretical route for designing superconducting materials.
main_problem: The primary goal is to elucidate the microscopic mechanism responsible for high-temperature superconductivity (HTSC) in cuprate superconductors, specifically focusing on $	ext{HgBa}_2	ext{Ca}_2	ext{Cu}_3	ext{O}_8$ ($	ext{Hg1223}$).
main_result: The pairing mechanism is identified as an emergent local attraction generated counter-intuitively from the strong bare local Coulomb repulsion ($U$), which contrasts with conventional BCS theory. The pressure dependence of $T_c$ is successfully modeled by the interplay between increased hopping ($t$), decreased $U$, and reduced off-site repulsion ($V$).
method: The study employs ab initio calculations solving low-energy effective Hamiltonians using advanced variational solvers supplemented by neural networks, alongside Variational Monte Carlo (VMC) methods.
model_or_system: The system is the triple-layer cuprate $	ext{Hg1223}$, modeled via an effective Hamiltonian incorporating onsite ($U$) and nearest-neighbor off-site ($V$) Coulomb interactions. The analysis distinguishes between inner ($	ext{IP}$) and outer ($	ext{OP}$) superconducting planes.
key_observables: Superconducting critical temperature ($T_c$), $d$-wave SC order parameter, spin structure factors ($S_s(q)$), and the effective attraction derived from onsite repulsion energy curvature.
important_parameters: Pressure (0 GPa to 60 GPa), doping concentration ($\delta$), hopping parameters ($t$), and Coulomb interactions ($U$, $V$).
main_assumptions: The methodology relies on extrapolating calculated properties to the thermodynamic limit ($L 	o \infty$) and assumes that the pairing mechanism can be captured by analyzing the energy dependence of onsite repulsion.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the dome-like pressure dependence of $T_c$ for $	ext{Hg1223}$, show the coexistence of SC and AF orders in different layers, and compare theoretical $T_c$ predictions against experimental data under applied pressure.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the Hamiltonian parameters ($t, U, V$) as a function of pressure. It first establishes the strong correlation origin of HTSC, then details how pressure modifies these interactions, leading to an emergent attraction that drives superconductivity, while also confirming the coexistence of SC and AF orders.
why_interesting: The identification of pairing via 'attraction from reduced repulsion' provides a novel theoretical framework for understanding high-$T_c$ superconductivity, linking it directly to correlation effects modulated by external pressure.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Crystal structure of Hg1223 and directions of primitive vectors (a, b, c). The inner CuO2 plane (IP) sandwiched by the two outer CuO2 planes (OPs) is separated from the equivalent next three CuO2 planes by the block layers, and the neighboring IP and OP are separated by interstitial Ca atoms. The crystal structure [6] is visualized from data in the Crystallography Open Database [7–9] using VESTA [10].

FIG. 2. Inner- and outer-layer hole densities as a function of total hole density 𝛿for the ab initio Hamiltonian of Hg1223 at ambient pressure. The IP always has lower hole density. The plots for 𝐿= 16 and 24 demonstrate negligible size dependence suggesting that the 𝐿= ∞limit is close.

FIG. 3. Doping concentration dependence of the SC order parameter 𝐹SC defined in Eq. (25) at ambient pressure. The red and blue lines show 𝐹SC for the IP and OP, respectively, after size extrapolation to the thermodynamic limit 𝐿→∞. The error bars are due to the size extrapolation error.

FIG. 4. Size dependent SC order parameters 𝐹SC for the IP and OP at ambient pressure as a function of hole density 𝛿IP and 𝛿OP in each layer for several choices of system sizes. In this figure, 𝐹SC is defined as the converged values of √𝑃𝑑at long distances before the size extrapolation.

FIG. 5. Spin structure factors defined in Eq. (17) (a) and SC corre- lation defined in Eq. (20) (b) of the IP and OP for 24 × 24 lattice. The chemical potential difference between the IP and OP is 𝜇= 3.2, which is decreased from the value 𝜇= 4.41 for the Hg1223 ab initio Hamiltonian at ambient pressure estimated from the GW calculation. The spin structure shows a prominent peak at Q = (𝜋, 𝜋) for the IP which may grow to the Bragg peak in the thermodynamic limit, whereas it shows no such peak for the OP. Both the IP and OP exhibit the SC correlation saturated at long distances. Because the AF corre- lation tends to degrade the SC correlation, the saturated value of the SC correlation is...
